We can check in this PR after you respond to the review comments without more progress on the `@rule` if you'd like. That's the benefit of this being under an experimental backend, we can split this up into small incremental PRs.

After that, I recommend splitting up the `@rule` implementation into a few steps:

1. Figure out exactly how you would run this command without using Pants, e.g. via `fpm` or `dpkg-deb`. What argv, for example?
2. Install the tool, either by expecting it to be already present via `BinaryPaths` or, if possible, installing it by using `TemplatedExternalTool`: https://www.pantsbuild.org/docs/rules-api-installing-tools#externaltool-install-pre-compiled-binaries. Test it works by getting `<tool> --help` working with `await Get(ProcessResult, Process())`.
3. Get the tool to build the simplest artifact possible. Don't worry yet about the whole `packages` field.
4. Wire up the `packages` field.

I recommend doing each of those steps incrementally, rather than trying to do it all at once. It definitely can be overwhelming seeing how all the pieces will fit together, so asking yourself "What's the simplest problem I can solve right now?" will help.

With each of those steps, I'm happy to go into more detail on how to do it :) only not giving more detail yet on any particular step to not overwhelm.

I believe there is spaceto improve user experience further beyond adding parity with nbclassic:
1. It's not clear what exactly "Close and Halt" and "Shut Down" items do and what is the difference between them. Please find suggestions below on renaming "Close and Halt" into "Close Notebook and Shutdown Kernel". "Shut Down" should then be renamed into "Shut Down Server". I think this can be done within this PR but can also be taken care of separately.
2. "Close and Halt"  terminates kernel of the notebook and "Shut Down" terminates server with all kernels accordingly. When this happens all unsaved data is lost. Therefore these buttons are destructive and can lead to data loss. To remedy this, we could ask a user to confirm his action via modal/popup window. We should then also add "..." to the end of both options to indicate that selecting the item will lead to further options or require additional input. It would probably be best to do it in a separate PR after Notebook 7 launch, please let me know if you agree with the approach and I can create an issue to track it.

I ran the `test_library_MovieSection_managedHubs` test locally and it was failing. I found out that you need to provide all three parameters (`promotedToRecommended`, `promotedToOwnHome`, and `promotedToSharedHome`) in the API call to update the visibility managed hubs, even if you don't change them. Currently `updateVisibility()` only provides the parameters that change instead of all three. This also means the `updateVisibility()` needs to automatically `reload()` the hub so that the default attributes get updated if the method is called again/chained.

https://github.com/codalab/codalab-worksheets/blob/master/frontend/src/components/worksheets/Worksheet/Worksheet.js#L666

The node here will be null (you can console.log the node) when we set the focus above the -1 item, since there is no item with id `codalab-worksheet-item--1`. A possible fix will to manually give the -1 item the id  codalab-worksheet-item--1 or some other special id, and find the item using that id

For AWG, I think using inheritance is good approach, especially if the instruments belong to same family. I see one minor problem that, in the inherited instrument, the name will remain "Agilent 33220A Arbitrary Waveform generator". It could possibly make sense to define a generic family class which defines all the common features and includes `name` parameter in `__init__`. The subclasses, one for each instrument, will define the correct name in `__init__`. In addition they will specify only the specific properties that are different.
A more structured approach would include a generic AWG instrument class from which an Agilent 322xxx generic family is inheriting from, which would finally be subclassed by specific Agilent AWG instruments. Here I would also highlight that the availability of dynamic property as proposed in #376 will avoid repetition in the inherited classes allowing to change, for instance, the frequency range by simply defining an attribute like `frequency_values=[1e-6,20e6]` or `frequency_values=[1e-6,80e6]` instead of redefining the complete property violating the DRY principle.

Overall, this is looking _really_ good. I quite like the direction it's gone and it's homing in on a sound solution.

I still worry there's just too much going on. This one change affects several different behaviors (introducing new keyring, switch to local ctypes API, changes existing pw checks, introduce encoding, introduces tests for broken encoding, ...). Although I think the final implementation may look very much like what you have here, it's just too much changing for one commit.

I'd much rather see this change as a series of incremental changes, each addressing more selective concerns, either as separate, independent PRs or as separate commits if very closely related.

For example: PR1 could implement simply switching from pywin32ctypes to a native ctypes implementation. That change could be reviewed, accepted, and released so that its effect could be proven and any unexpected regressions could be addressed before accepting more changes that depend on that change. Then PR2 could implement the sharding strategy (based on PR1). PR3 could fix the existing password checks and be independent of PR1 and PR3.

How would you feel about that approach?

This PR doesn't support version 1 and 2 of FML network protocols. You can use `docker run -it -e EULA=true -e TYPE=FORGE -e VERSION=1.20.1 -p 25565:25565 --name mc --rm itzg/minecraft-server` (add `:java8` to the end for 1.12 and 1.13) for testing this PR, here are my results:

- Forge 1.12 (FML v1) - `JavaStatusResponse.forge_data` is `None`. Reason: `forgeData` was named as `modinfo` before 1.13.
- Forge 1.13 (FML v2) - `KeyError: 'modid'`. Reason: `RawForgeDataMod.modid` was renamed to `modId` (capital `I`) in v2.
- Forge 1.20 (FML v3) - works.

Please, fix support for all versions of FML network protocol.

**Design question:** At the API level, could we consolidate `prefetch_blocks` and `async_fetch_batch` with a single `prefetch_batches` argument that gives the number of batches to asynchronously prefetch?

## Motivation

I think this will be a lot cleaner from an end-user perspective, with a single argument controlling prefetching behavior that has clear batch-based semantics. We ultimately care about **pipelining upstream batch preparation with downstream batch computation**, and both prefetching batches over the network and background batch slicing/concatenation/formatting fall under the former, so having a single batch-based argument controlling how many batches we want to prepare while a single batch is being processed seems like better UX. IMO, the object store transfer prefetching should probably be an implementation detail in facilitating a generic "how many batches to we want to prepare in the background on this worker" API, similar to the semantics of the [`tf.data` prefetching API](https://www.tensorflow.org/api_docs/python/tf/data/Dataset#prefetch).

## Do We Need Two Knobs?

Since this seems like nicer UX, the only reasons that we'd want to have two knobs (that I can think of) are:
1. We want to have different levels of prefetching for object store transfers compared to background slicing/concatenation/format conversion.
2. The prefetching unit for the two args should be different (e.g. batch vs. block).

(1) could be true. If we assume that we're not hitting NIC bandwidth or CPU bottlenecks (e.g. deserialization or batch prep), then prefetching object store transfers is ultimately limited by the node's object store capacity, while background slicing/concatenation/format conversion is ultimately limited by the node's worker heap, which are separate resource pools with different capacities. This is further complicated by format conversions leading to memory inflation. E.g. for a Pandas batch format UDF on Arrow blocks executed on a node that has equal object store and worker heap allocations, you could imagine that we want to prefetch 8 batches to the object store, but only do 1 background batch slice and format conversion since the Pandas format conversion results in a DataFrame that's 4x larger than the Arrow data (and the UDF will need to at least allocate an output in the worker heap as well).

However, this may be a non-issue if:
* the Python worker heap is typically the bottleneck rather than the node's object store (I think this is true for transformations/trainers that don't use a NumPy/Arrow narrow waist), or;
* we can rely on Ray Core's object pull manager to do proper backpressure on object transfers (I believe this is true).

The user can set `prefetch_batches` tailored to whatever eliminates compute resource stalls subject to a Python worker heap cap, and object transfer prefetching will do a best-effort fulfillment of `prefetch_batches`, subject to Ray Core's backpressure on object transfers when the node's object store pressure gets high. I'm thinking that this might make (1) a non-issue.

(2) is the status quo, but I think that the current object store prefetching API would be better expressed in terms of batches (this has been a long-standing TODO), so I don't think that (2) is a concern.

## Semantics and Implementation

For `.map_batches()`, this `prefetch_batches` arg would have the existing `async_fetch_batch` semantics, except that the buffer size is configurable. Down the line, we might have this arg control the number of blocks that are prefetched in the actor compute model.

For `.iter_batches()`, this `prefetch_batches` arg would have the existing `async_fetch_batch` semantics along with exposing the buffer size, _and_ would control how many blocks are prefetched into the object store on the consumer node. Namely, if we know the number of rows in a block from the readily-available block metadata (this should be defined in the common case), we would prefetch `N` blocks such that `N` is the smallest number of blocks that satisfies `prefetch_batches`, i.e. we choose an `N` such that `sum([b.num_rows for b in blocks[:N]) >= prefetch_batches * batch_size` and `sum([b.num_rows for b in blocks[:N-1]) < prefetch_batches * batch_size`. If the block metadata doesn't have the number of rows in the block (e.g. the dataset is a lazy read of CSV files, for which we don't know the number of rows until the entire files has been read), then we can prefetch one block at a time.

@ArturNiederfahrenhorst I just did a more thorough pass on your LSTM tests. The test is not really testing the rollout of LSTM cells during training (test_forward_train)

What you are doing right now:
- stacking obs, actions, and states in multiple BATCHES. Where is the time dimension then?
- This is like using LSTM cells as a fully connected cell :) 

What you should be doing:
- stack obs, and actions in both batches and times. So that the final shape of batch is (bxt, ho) and the final shape of actions is (bxt, ha)
- stack the state_in to be of shape (b, n_layer=1, hs) 
- if the number of steps are not compatible with (b, t) reshaping you need to zero pad. For example if you took 30 steps and your max_rollout_len = 20. You need to create a (1, 20, ho) obs tensor and concat if with a (1, 10, ho) obs. You cannot. So you need to zero pad the second one to make it (1, 20, ho) and take care of zeros with masking. the obs shape in this case should be come (2x20, ho) with bunch of zeros in there. In this example your state_in shape should be (2, 1, hs). 

Makes sense? I guess what you are testing assumes every sequence is of length 1 (max_rollout_length=1). That's why you don't see any errors.
I would recommend taking out the lstm stuff in forward_train from your test. your rollout test seems correct. 

Do the lstm forward_train in a separate method and PR. I can do that too and you can keep your focus on catalog stuff?

Hi @AllanOXDi. @AlexVelezLl makes a correct point, and his suggestion about making sure your adjustments don't affect other window sizes is a good idea. 

The `$toolbar-height` variable you change is used in several places and for some other calculations, so there will likely be unintended consequences of changing it - like in the desktop view of the topics page. Rather than using media queries, which is a common strategy that Alex suggests, you can use our `responsiveWindowMixin` which you can see used in this file already. It accomplishes the same thing, by using KDS. The `windowIsLarge`, `windowIsMedium`, and `windowIsSmall` values that you may have seen referenced throughout the Kolibri frontend come from this mixin (which is imported as [part of KDS](https://kolibri-design-system.netlify.app/layout/#responsiveness)). These are designated "breakpoints" which we use across the app for consistency. `windowIsLarge`, `windowIsMedium`, and `windowIsSmall` return a Boolean. This bug is a great way to learn more about how KDS helps with managing responsive design, as we use this extensively in the Kolibri front-end.

One way you could incorporate this mixin into your fix might be to set a condition if `windowIsSmall` is true. You can use a [ternary operator](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Operators/Conditional_Operator) pattern of [setting the class based on if a particular condition is true that follows this example](https://github.com/learningequality/kolibri/blob/develop/kolibri/core/assets/src/views/ExamReport/index.vue#L95), and then in your styles, define two different classes - one to be used for small windows, and one to be used otherwise. That way, you don't need to change the variable at all, and can specify the spacing specifically for small/mobile views. Let me know what questions you might have!

There is one general issue with "test-case parametrizations" such as in [ogc module](https://github.com/sentinel-hub/sentinelhub-py/blob/19b82df5cc27d31ed36cff212a84cdf00028b2e6/tests/test_ogc.py#L50). Classes `OgcRequest`, `WmsReqest`, `WcsRequest`, `AwsRequest`, etc. actually already make quite some work at the initialization. They prepare a list of requests for files that will have to be downloaded. To create these lists they even have to make some small info requests to e.g. WFS service. This means that before tests start officially running a lot of things already happens (which you can also see from the delay when you execute pytest command). If during this time something would fail, e.g some service would be unavailable or we made some bug, then everything would fail without a good error message.

To improve this, all sh-py classes would have to be initialized during actual test execution. A trick how to do this might be that you replace all class initializations, e.g. `WmsRequest(data_collection=...)`, with simple `dict` initializations, e.g. `dict(data_collection=...)`. Then somewhere during each testcase run you initialize e.g. `WmsRequest` with parameters from the dictionary. I noticed that such a solution has already been used [here](https://github.com/sentinel-hub/sentinelhub-py/blob/19b82df5cc27d31ed36cff212a84cdf00028b2e6/tests/test_geopedia.py#L95).

I know it will again be quite some work but I think it would significantly improve the testing procedure.

I think this looks good!  I really like the mapping concept, and it's obviously necessary to build out the partition-based lineage.

Some high-level thoughts:  
Should the mapping class be aware both directions of the mapping, or just one?  To map parent/child lineage, we could instantiate one bi-directional mapping or two uni-directional mappings.

To do any operational tasks, we also need to have some sort of mapping class to go from asset partitions to computational partitions.  There's nothing that tells me that we shouldn't just use this same mapping class, but its less clear that it's a parent/child relationship (which makes more sense in a lineage concept).

When we specify the partition mapping on each node, isn't it possible now to build up an inconsistent partition dependency map?  I would think that you would actually want to calculate the mapping only on the child.  Are we going to error when we get inconsistencies?

> @bmw I have second thoughts about the naming pattern `Generic<ClassName>` for the public `TypeVar` instances introduced in the project. Given that the `TypeVar` itself is not a generic, but the variable of a generic representing "any" possible type of a given bound, I think a better name would be `Any<ClassName>` (eg. `AnyJSONObjectWithField`, `AnyImmutableMap`).
>
> But if is too late, I can live with `Generic<ClassName>` and prefer to keep a coherent convention for `GenericTypedJSONObjectWithFields`.

Sorry I missed this question earlier. I personally don't love the `Any*` name. In this PR, the return value for `register` isn't **any** `TypedJSONObjectWithFields` and is instead the specific subclass passed in as the parameter. Maybe `TypedJSONObjectWithFieldsGeneric`, `TypedJSONObjectWithFieldsType`, or `TypedJSONObjectWithFieldsTypeVar` is better?

I personally am in favor of just sticking with our current convention though. It's not perfect, but I think the name "Generic" helps communicate it is a Python typing generic and including the class name helps communicate the bounds of the type.

Overall looks good to me but I have 2 comments here: 

1) Needs rebase on top of latest in order to trigger tests

2) Everything that uses the `Attachment` class will not work with your overriden/custom class that will inherit from `BaseAttachment`. At the very minimum I see: `delete_stale_attachments.py`, `attachments_tags.py`, `admin.py`, `forms.py` (used in views). 

This second item pretty much breaks the entire out-of-the box experience of django-attachments because all of these places contain hard-coded references to the `Attachment` model itself instead of whatever custom model the developer provides in their app. 

So IMO this can go in either of 2 directions: 

1) If you are building a custom app and care only about the model and not really anything else (iow you are handling all of the above mentioned functionality yourself) inheriting directly from `Attachment` and having another DB table should not be a big deal IMO. 

2) If you are aiming to provide a fully customizable experience for the developer this PR needs more work (could be split into smaller PRs which are easier to review). Something like:
  - add a setting and remove hard-coded references; possibly refactor tests a bit so they consume the setting. Similar to what Django does for the User model.
  - add the abstract BaseAttachment class
  - document the changes and how others can use them
  - add a custom attachment model class in the test suite and retest everything with it as well (could be a parameter to avoid code duplication). 
 - fix any bugs if present

This is a potential issue with many uses of `InfoExtractor._download_json()` where `fatal=True` is the default but a junk result (not a `dict`, or an empty `dict`, or a `dict` with unexpected contents) may still be returned.

Wouldn't it be better to extend or modify this core method instead?

The documentation says it returns a `dict`, so if the JSON parses to `False` it should be treated as an error according to the `fatal` value, either return `{}` (possibly `None`: the method doesn't have a `default` parameter), or `raise ExtractorError`.  _On reflection, the documentation is probably wrong in detail as any valid output of `json.loads()`, not just `dict`, can be returned, but `False` could still be treated as an error._

In case that might break existing code, we could instead add a new keyword parameter (say) `validator=None`, similar to `transform_source`, that, if specified, will be applied to the result as a function (in this example, one could use `lambda x: x['title']`) and according to `fatal`, either return False or `raise ExtractorError` if it raises.

Hm, this is currently specific to the basic variant generator (which might be ok), but I have a slightly different approach to discuss:

Currently:

- Categorical samples indices instead of values
- Categorical resolves values in a separate API call

Why?

- Because we actually pre-generate all samples in the `_VariantIterator`

Problems:

- This is not easily generalizable to other searchers
- It's not very easy to overwrite the configs for existing trials, as the Trial.config objects will still have the actual object in them and not the index.


Instead, maybe:

- Scan parameter space for any Categoricals. Replace Categorical.categories with a same-shape list of `_Placeholder<path, index>` objects
- _Placeholder objects could include the string representation of the object (e.g. for the trial table)
- We can choose to only replace objects of types we care about (e.g. Datasets, object refs), and not for primitives
- Create a map of placeholders to "real" categoricals, e.g. `placeholder_to_obj: Dict[_Placeholder, Any]`
- Remember which keys had categoricals, e.g. `categorical_keys: Set[Tuple[str, ...]`
- After sampling, replace every sampled placeholder object with the respective object from `placeholder_to_obj`
- On restore, we only need to update `placeholder_to_obj`

Benefits:
- This won't need any adjustment to the sampling of Categoricals, and it can be wrapped around any searcher.suggest() call. So it generealizes to every searcher.
- Also we can just use the placeholders to update Trial.config objects post restore

For functions, we could either build the replacement map on the fly or just not support it for restoration. I think not supporting it is actually ok.


What do you think?

> These scopes match those in pushmsixscript. Now that I see them in this list, I wonder, should they contain ":firefox"?

I give you a solid "maybe".

It makes sense if we're planning on submitting other things to the microsoft store, and we want to limit what can be pushed there.

If we're pushing, say, thunderbird or mobile or something to the store, then that will be a separate trust domain and a separate set of pools, so splitting out `:firefox` may or may not help, especially if we have a different set of store creds per pool. We could add the trust domain here, e.g. `:gecko`, if that's what we're guarding against.

If we're planning on pushing multiple products from the same trust domain, and we're releasing, say, devedition from beta, but on the release track, then absolutely this makes sense.

Then again, if we don't know of any such requirement, we can:

a) roll out as-is
b) find out that there is another product we want to ship off a different set of branches in the same trust domain,
c) deprecate the old scopes,
d) add a new set of scopes that is more granular, add support for the new scopes in the pools, restrict the appropriate scopes, and roll out, then
e) when the new scopes have ridden the trains or have been uplifted, remove the deprecated scopes.

So if we know of other uses of this worker coming shortly, we may want to adjust now. If we may someday need more uses, we can adjust the scopes as needed when we know more about the requirements. Long story short: I think we're probably fine for now unless there's something coming I'm not aware of.
